name: Swastika
shortDescription: An ancient Hindu symbol representing well-being, prosperity, auspiciousness, and good fortune; a sacred mark deeply embedded in Hindu traditions.
description: The Swastika is an ancient symbol deeply revered in Hinduism, representing well-being, prosperity, auspiciousness, and good fortune. Its earliest known usage dates back to the Indus Valley Civilization and it is frequently mentioned in the Vedas. The word 'Swastika' comes from the Sanskrit 'svasti,' meaning 'well-being.' The symbol consists of a cross with arms bent at right angles, each arm pointing in the same rotary direction. The Swastika is commonly found adorning Hindu temples, religious texts, wedding invitations, doorways, and other objects and places considered sacred. It symbolizes the sun, Vishnu, Brahma, and cyclical nature of the universe, depending on the context and sect. Different orientations and slight variations in design can subtly alter the specific interpretation, but the core meaning remains overwhelmingly positive and auspicious. Its presence is believed to ward off evil and attract good luck.
subtitle: A Symbol of Auspiciousness and Prosperity

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: The Swastika's origins trace back to the Indus Valley Civilization (c. 3300-1700 BCE), where archaeological evidence suggests its usage. It appears in various forms on seals and artifacts from this period. In the Vedic tradition, the Swastika is associated with several deities and concepts. Some interpretations connect it to Surya, the sun god, representing his daily journey across the sky. Other interpretations relate it to Vishnu, the preserver, or to Brahma, the creator, signifying the cosmos' cyclical nature. The symbol's presence in ancient texts like the Rigveda and Upanishads indicates its early and enduring significance in Hindu religious and philosophical thought. The direction of the arms is also subject to interpretation, sometimes associated with the clockwise motion of creation and preservation, and other times with the counter-clockwise motion of destruction and transformation.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Swastika plays a vital role in Hindu rituals and ceremonies. It is considered highly auspicious to draw or display the symbol during festivals, weddings, and other important events. It is often placed at the entrance of homes and temples to welcome positive energy and ward off negativity. The Swastika is also an integral part of yantras, geometric diagrams used in meditation and worship. Its presence in religious texts and scriptures underscores its importance as a sacred symbol. The Swastika's association with prosperity and well-being makes it a popular motif in Hindu art and architecture, where it is frequently incorporated into designs and decorations. It is also a common sight on Hindu businesses and shops, believed to bring good luck and success.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Swastika's symbolism is multifaceted, encompassing various interpretations within Hinduism. Primarily, it represents auspiciousness, good fortune, and prosperity. Its association with the sun god Surya symbolizes light, energy, and life. The clockwise orientation (卐), known as 'Swastika,' is often linked to creation, progress, and positive energy. The counter-clockwise orientation (卍), known as 'Sauvastika', can represent night, destruction, or the feminine principle. Some interpret the four arms as representing the four Vedas (Rigveda, Samaveda, Yajurveda, and Atharvaveda) or the four aims of human life (Dharma, Artha, Kama, and Moksha). Others see them as representing the four directions. Ultimately, the Swastika's symbolism is deeply rooted in the Hindu worldview, emphasizing the interconnectedness of all things and the cyclical nature of existence.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Despite its unfortunate appropriation by the Nazi regime in the 20th century, the Swastika remains a deeply sacred symbol for Hindus and other Dharmic religions. Efforts continue to educate the public about the symbol's true origins and positive connotations, seeking to reclaim it from the stigma associated with hate and genocide. Within the Hindu community, the Swastika continues to be widely used and revered, serving as a reminder of ancient traditions and spiritual values. Its continued presence in temples, homes, and cultural events underscores its enduring significance. The symbol's legacy is complex, marked by both reverence and controversy, but its core meaning of auspiciousness and well-being persists for millions of Hindus worldwide. Hindu organizations actively work to promote awareness of the Swastika's true meaning and history, aiming to combat ignorance and prejudice.

sources.author: Klaus Klostermaier
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive overview of Hinduism, including the significance of the Swastika symbol.

sources.author: Alain Danielou
sources.description: Explores the symbolism and mythology of Hindu deities and symbols, including the Swastika.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Represents light, energy, and life-giving force

associations.name: Prosperity
associations.significance: Symbol of wealth, abundance, and good fortune

associations.name: Auspiciousness
associations.significance: Represents good omens and positive beginnings.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Thor's Hammer (Mjölnir)
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Symbol of protection and good fortune, though with different visual representation and origin.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Sun Wheel
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Represents the sun, cyclical movement, and life-giving energy.

companions.name: Lakshmi
companions.description: Goddess of wealth, prosperity, and good fortune, often depicted alongside the Swastika.

companions.name: Ganesha
companions.description: God of beginnings, wisdom, and remover of obstacles, often invoked with the Swastika.

worshipPractices.name: Drawing on entrances
worshipPractices.description: The Swastika is drawn on the entrances of homes and businesses to invite prosperity and ward off negative energy.

worshipPractices.name: Placement on religious objects
worshipPractices.description: The Swastika is placed on religious objects, such as books and statues, to imbue them with auspiciousness.

festivals.name: Diwali
festivals.description: The Swastika is prominently displayed during Diwali, the festival of lights, to welcome prosperity and good fortune.

festivals.name: Akshaya Tritiya
festivals.description: Akshaya Tritiya, considered a highly auspicious day, sees widespread use of the Swastika in rituals and ceremonies.

cultural.modernLegacy: Despite historical misuse, the Swastika remains a sacred and widely used symbol in Hindu communities worldwide. Ongoing efforts aim to reclaim and educate about its true meaning.
